Rattanarangsan Palace (Ranong)

**Ranong**[[c]](#cite_note-7) is one of [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand")'s southern [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Thailand "Provinces of Thailand") (*changwat*), on the west coast along the [Andaman Sea](/wiki/Andaman_Sea "Andaman Sea"). It has the fewest inhabitants of all Thai provinces (making it the least populous of all the Thai provinces). Provinces neighboring Ranong are (clockwise) [Chumphon](/wiki/Chumphon_province "Chumphon province"), [Surat Thani](/wiki/Surat_Thani_province "Surat Thani province"), and [Phang Nga](/wiki/Phang_Nga_province "Phang Nga province"). To the west, it borders [Kawthaung](/wiki/Kawthaung "Kawthaung"), [Tanintharyi](/wiki/Tanintharyi_Region "Tanintharyi Region"), [Myanmar](/wiki/Burma "Burma").

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ranong_province&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Geography")]

Ranong is on the [Kra Isthmus](/wiki/Kra_Isthmus "Kra Isthmus"), a narrow strip of land, only 44 kilometres (27 mi) wide,[[5]](#cite_note-BP-20180719-8) that connects Thailand with the [Malay Peninsula](/wiki/Malay_Peninsula "Malay Peninsula"), on the west of the [Phuket mountain range](/wiki/Phuket_mountain_range "Phuket mountain range"). It has a long coast on the [Andaman Sea](/wiki/Andaman_Sea "Andaman Sea"). The province, together with [Trat province](/wiki/Trat_province#Climate "Trat province"), is known for being one of the wettest places in Thailand, the rainy season lasting for about eight months.[[6]](#cite_note-9)

Ranong is the least populated province. The total forest area is 1,726 km2 (666 sq mi) or 53.5 percent of provincial area, and 67 percent is mountainous.[[7]](#cite_note-10) In former years the major industry was [tin](/wiki/Tin "Tin") mining, but most mines are now exhausted. White clay mining (for the production of porcelain) and fishing are now the main industries, along with rubber and [cashew nuts](/wiki/Cashew_nut "Cashew nut").

The Ngao Mangrove Forest Research Centre, also known as the Ranong Biosphere Reserve, in the north of [Kapoe District](/wiki/Kapoe_District "Kapoe District"), covering 189,431 [rai](/wiki/Rai_(unit) "Rai (unit)")[[5]](#cite_note-BP-20180719-8) (303.09 km2), was declared in 1997. It is the fourth biosphere reserve of Thailand, and the only one on the seacoast protecting mangrove forests.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ranong_province&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: History")]

Ranong province once was a deputy town of [Chumphon](/wiki/Chumphon "Chumphon") in the [Ayutthaya era](/wiki/Ayutthaya_Kingdom "Ayutthaya Kingdom") (1350–1767).[[9]](#cite_note-:0-12) It was a small, mountainous town that protected Chumphon from Burma. The population was very low until the discovery of tin, when people from many cities and foreigners started to visit Ranong to do tin business there. This also gave rise to the name Ranong, which was a distorted form of *rae nong* (แร่นอง), meaning "full of minerals".[[10]](#cite_note-13) The ruler of Ranong was known after the town as *Luang Ranong* ([Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): หลวงระนอง)

At the start of the [Rattanakosin era](/wiki/Rattanakosin_Kingdom_(1782%E2%80%931932) "Rattanakosin Kingdom (1782–1932)") (1781–present), a wealthy Chinese merchant, [Kho Su Jiang](/wiki/Kho_Su_Jiang "Kho Su Jiang") bought a majority share of the tin business in Ranong and was appointed tax collector of the region, with the title *Luang Rattana Setti* ([Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): หลวงรัตนเศรษฐี), by King [Nangklao](/wiki/Rama_III "Rama III") (Rama III). In 1854, the *Luang Ranong* died. King [Mongkut](/wiki/Mongkut "Mongkut") (Rama IV) then promoted Kho Su Jiang to the position, promoting him to *Phra Rattana Setti* ([Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): พระรัตนเศรษฐี). By then, Ranong's wealth had increased greatly and through taxation was a significant source of income for the capital, thus in 1877 King [Chulalongkorn](/wiki/Chulalongkorn "Chulalongkorn") (Rama V) elevated Ranong to provincial status, answering directly to Bangkok as opposed to Chumphon, with Phra Rattana Setti becoming its first governor.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-12)

After the [First Anglo-Burmese War](/wiki/First_Anglo-Burmese_War "First Anglo-Burmese War"), when the British assumed control of [Tenasserim](/wiki/Tanintharyi_Region "Tanintharyi Region") and the [Kraburi River](/wiki/Kraburi_River "Kraburi River") became the demarcation line between Siam and British-controlled Burma, Ranong became an important border city where goods were exchanged, a status that it still holds today.

Being located on the [Kra Isthmus](/wiki/Kra_Isthmus "Kra Isthmus"), the narrowest part (44 kilometres) of the [Malay Peninsula](/wiki/Malay_Peninsula "Malay Peninsula"), a proposal of [a canal](/wiki/Thai_Canal "Thai Canal") through the area has been long suggested, particularly by the French. If successful, traveling from Europe to China would be faster through this route instead of circumnavigating the [Malay Peninsula](/wiki/Malay_Peninsula "Malay Peninsula"). This was seen as a threat to the financial growth of the harbors of [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore_in_the_Straits_Settlements "Singapore in the Straits Settlements") and [Penang](/wiki/Penang "Penang"), which at the time were owned by Britain, who therefore forced the suspension of the project. At the end of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), Thailand signed a British-imposed treaty called "The Termination of The State of War Between Siam and Allies", which forbade Thailand from digging such a canal without British permission. This treaty was revoked in 1954.[[11]](#cite_note-14) Since then, plans for the canal have continued to be suggested and discussed, even in the present day, though [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Thailand "Prime Minister of Thailand") [Prayut Chan-o-cha](/wiki/Prayut_Chan-o-cha "Prayut Chan-o-cha") said in 2018 that it was not a government priority.[[12]](#cite_note-15)

Ranong has also been historically significant as a residence for royal tours of the southern provinces, with many kings of Thailand visiting Ranong in the past. The first was King Chulalongkorn, who stayed at Rattana Ransan palace for three nights in 1890. The palace is now a landmark in Ranong. King [Prajadhipok](/wiki/Prajadhipok "Prajadhipok") (Rama VII) visited in 1928, and King [Bhumibol](/wiki/Bhumibol_Adulyadej "Bhumibol Adulyadej") (Rama IX) and Queen [Sirikit](/wiki/Sirikit "Sirikit") visited in 1959.

## Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ranong_province&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Climate")]

Most of Thailand receives from 1,200 to 1,600 mm of precipitation per year. Two provinces, Ranong and [Trat](/wiki/Trat_province "Trat province"), receive more than 4,500 mm a year making them the wettest places in the country.[[13]](#cite_note-16)

For this reason, Ranong is called *"Mueang Fon Paet Daet Si"* (เมืองฝนแปดแดดสี่, pronounced [[mɯːaŋ fǒn pɛ̀ːt dɛ̀ːt sìː]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Thai "Help:IPA/Thai"), lit. 'the city of eight months of rain and four months of sunshine'), meaning it rains for eight months of the year, and only four months are without rain.[[14]](#cite_note-17)

## Symbols

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ranong_province&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Symbols")]

The [provincial seal](/wiki/Seals_of_the_provinces_of_Thailand "Seals of the provinces of Thailand") shows a castle on top of a hill, as an aide memoire that King [Chulalongkorn](/wiki/Chulalongkorn "Chulalongkorn") (Rama V) once visited Ranong and stayed at the Ratana Rangsan Castle on top of the Niveskiri Hill. The number five refers to King Rama V; the castle is Ratana Rangsan Palace; the mountain is Niveshkiri; the royal tray refers to the people of Ranong.[[15]](#cite_note-OSMA-18)

The provincial slogan is, "Kra Isthmus, mountain grass, cashew nut, mineral water stream, and real pearl of Ranong."[[15]](#cite_note-OSMA-18)

The [provincial tree](/wiki/List_of_Thai_provincial_trees "List of Thai provincial trees") is the [Banaba](/wiki/Lagerstroemia_speciosa "Lagerstroemia speciosa") (*Lagerstroemia speciosa*), and the provincial flower is the [orchid](/wiki/Orchid "Orchid") *[Dendrobium formosum](/wiki/Dendrobium_formosum "Dendrobium formosum")*. The [endemic](/wiki/Endemic "Endemic") [terrestrial crab](/wiki/Terrestrial_crab "Terrestrial crab") *[Phricotelphusa sirindhorn](/w/index.php?title=Phricotelphusa_sirindhorn&action=edit&redlink=1 "Phricotelphusa sirindhorn (page does not exist)")* is the provincial aquatic life.

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ranong_province&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Transportation")]

[Phet Kasem Road](/wiki/Phet_Kasem_Road "Phet Kasem Road") (Thailand Route 4) runs through the province. [Ranong Airport](/wiki/Ranong_Airport "Ranong Airport") is about 24 kilometres (15 mi) south of [Ranong](/wiki/Ranong "Ranong").

The [Port Authority of Thailand](/wiki/Port_Authority_of_Thailand "Port Authority of Thailand") operates the Ranong Port, which is Thailand's principal [Indian Ocean](/wiki/Indian_Ocean "Indian Ocean") port. In 2008, the [Ranong human-smuggling incident](/wiki/Ranong_human-smuggling_incident "Ranong human-smuggling incident") resulted in 54 deaths.[[20]](#cite_note-23)

## Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ranong_province&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Tourism")]

**Namtok Ngao National Park** (อุทยานแห่งชาติน้ำตกหงาว) Covering a total area of approximately 417,500 [rai](/wiki/Rai_(area) "Rai (area)") (668 km2) it was declared a national park on 3 June 1999.[[21]](#cite_note-24)

**Ko Chang (Ranong)** (เกาะช้าง) island in tambon Ko Phayam. With an area of 18 square kilometres and 80 homes on the entire island. Cashew and para rubber plantations as well as coastal fisheries are the main industries.[[22]](#cite_note-25)

**Laem Son National Park** (อุทยานแห่งชาติแหลมสน) It covers a total area of 196,875 [rai](/wiki/Rai_(area) "Rai (area)") (31.5 hectares). It also includes islands in the Andaman Sea; namely, Ko Khangkhao and Mu Ko Kam.[[23]](#cite_note-26) Places of interest within the national park include:

* **Hat Bang Ben** (หาดบางเบน) is a long sandy beach shaded by pine trees where the national park headquarters is located.
* **Hat Laem Son** (หาดแหลมสน) is a white sandy beach approximately four kilometres next to Hat Bang Ben.
* **Hat Praphat** or **Hat Hin Thung** (หาดประพาส หรือ หาดหินทุ่ง) Similar to Hat Bang Ben, Hat Praphat is a large beach shaded by pine trees.
* **Ko Khangkhao** (เกาะค้างคาว) is an island with fine sandy beaches and a gravel beach known as Hat Hin Ngam in the north.
* **Ko Kam Yai** (เกาะกำใหญ่) is a scenic island with white sandy beaches.
* **Ko Kam Nui** (เกาะกำนุ้ย) is not far from Ko Kam Yai with beaches on one side and many other nearby islets.

**Raksawarin Hot Springs and Public Park** (บ่อน้ำพุร้อนรักษะวาริน) The most famous and popular [hot spring](/wiki/Hot_spring "Hot spring") of Ranong. It have been analysed by the Department of Science Service to contain important minerals, and it is the only source in Thailand that does not contain any sulphur additives. It is possible to drink from the source, and it is also regarded as pure water. The hot springs and public park is in Ranong town.[[24]](#cite_note-27)

**Phu Khao Ya** (ภูเขาหญ้า) The bald mountain with grass growing all over the mountain, it is extraordinary in that it is a geographical anomaly. This average-sized hill is entirely void of trees leaving it looking, as local residents say, like a "Swiss pasture". Phu Khao Ya can also change color according to the season and time of day. It is situated 12 kilometres (7.4 mi) to the south of Ranong town.[[25]](#cite_note-28)

**Khlong Naka** (คลองนาคา) This wildlife sanctuary is the only home for the aquatic plant *[Crinum thaianum](/wiki/Crinum_thaianum "Crinum thaianum")*. This species of plant is [endemic](/wiki/Endemic "Endemic") to the local stream, and from October till November is when the flowers bloom at their most.[[26]](#cite_note-29)

**Wat Hat Som Paen** (วัดหาดส้มแป้น) The local Ranong [Buddhist temple](/wiki/Wat "Wat"). Its highlight, besides worshiping the Buddha, is also admiring the beauty of the stream that flows through the temple, which is also home to a large number of the [rock mahseer](/wiki/Neolissochilus_stracheyi "Neolissochilus stracheyi").[[27]](#cite_note-30)
